title: How AI could help diagnose a deadly heart condition

Atrial fibrillation affects occurs when the heart goes into a sudden, random period of excitation. Combining electrocardiogram measurements with AI could help to detect it, say scientists.Credit: Leonello Calvetti/Science Photo Library/GettyThe electrocardiogram (ECG) is a powerful tool for both diagnosing many cardiovascular diseases and assessing the risk of developing them in the future. However, atrial fibrillation and some other cardiovascular conditions exhibit symptoms only intermittently, making it challenging to capture and assess risk from a 10-second ECG readout.A common form of arrhythmia, atrial fibrillation affects around a million people in Japan, and about 3% of adults globally. It occurs when the heart goes into a sudden, random period of excitation. During such spells, the heart may skip contractions, which can disrupt blood flow. This disruption can cause clots to form in the bloodstream, which can travel to key arteries and cause a stroke.“Atrial fibrillation increases the risk of stroke, heart failure and premature death,” says Tetsuo Sasano, a cardiologist and electrophysiologist at the Institute of Science Tokyo in Japan.Atrial fibrillation’s sporadic nature makes it challenging to detect. “Atrial fibrillation is notoriously difficult to diagnose because of its transient, or sometimes even non-existent, symptoms,” says Sasano.Game changerThat is unfortunate because it can be treated if picked up. “We have the means to effectively treat atrial fibrillation,” adds Sasano. “And so, finding a way to accurately assess risk and diagnose the disease quickly would be a game changer for many patients.”The current method of capturing evidence of an atrial-fibrillation attack is for patients to wear an ECG monitor for a week or two. But even then, a definitive diagnosis may be missed.But even with long-term ECG monitoring, catching atrial fibrillation can be a matter of luck, explains Sasano. “A major problem is that the presence or absence of atrial fibrillation cannot be predicted from a standard 12-lead ECG,” he says. “Trained experts may not always see the signs of atrial fibrillation in an ECG readout, because the disease induces subtle electrical and structural changes that are invisible to the human eye.”However, AI may offer a promising way to address this problem, he argues: “This is where AI really comes into its own. A well-trained model can learn to spot these subtle, almost imperceptible, signals from just a few seconds worth of ECG signal tracing.”To this end, scientists at medical device manufacturer Fukuda Denshi Co. Ltd, in Tokyo, with physicians at local medical institutions, explored an AI model they hoped could diagnose atrial fibrillation, or predict the risk of developing the disease, from a single, 10-second ECG session.This led to the development of the first approved AI-driven ECG in Japan, says Sasano. Crucially, the AI component comes as an app that is fully compatible with existing ECG machines, eliminating the need for extra hardware, expensive upgrades to existing equipment or complex training1.While the technology has been approved for use in Japan, it is yet to be approved in other regions such as the United Kingdom, European Union and the United States.Harnessing ECG’s powerThe researchers created an application package that could easily be integrated into an existing ECG machine, providing doctors with rapid diagnoses and risk assessments on the screen.To build the AI model that would underpin the app, the researchers collected retrospective ECG data from 2,930 outpatients aged 40 years and over from seven cardiology clinics across Japan2. These patients, who all had a history of cardiovascular problems, were divided into two groups — those with known atrial fibrillation and a control group without an atrial fibrillation diagnosis. Atrial-fibrillation status was confirmed by clinical diagnosis and rhythm documentation under cardiologist supervision. None of the patients received anti-arrhythmia drugs during the study period.Researchers at Fukuda Denshi taking an electrocardiogram of a person (right) and investigating how AI can be used to analyse the readout (left).Credit: Fukuda“We needed data from different patient groups so that we could train our AI model to read various ECG outputs and spot signs of atrial fibrillation,” says Sasano. “The control group was key — we wanted to see if any of these patients had hidden atrial fibrillation, and we asked the model to search through their ECG results to identify them.”The best-trained model achieved robust and accurate results, correctly identifying over 75% of atrial fibrillation cases in training datasets.“Our AI model can read, analyse and predict atrial fibrillation risks from a standard sinus ECG readout in around two seconds,” says Hongmin Wu, principal technology development specialist at Fukuda Denshi, who led the technical development of the the model2. “Once we had optimized the AI model for cardiovascular patients, we were able to secure approval from the Pharmaceuticals and Medical Devices Agency here in Japan.”The app has been installed on ECG machines in several Japanese hospitals since 2024.“Some participating facilities reported that the AI-driven risk estimation led to confirmed atrial-fibrillation diagnoses that would otherwise have been missed,” says Wu. “By enabling physicians to prioritize high-risk patient who require long-term monitoring, our system serves as a critical tool in the new era of AI-assisted cardiology.”AI for wider diagnosticsSo far, the training data the researchers have used for the technology is only based on populations in Japan, and they say it needs to be tested in other populations before the app is expanded to other regions of the world and used more widely in Japan.An electrocardiogram device that uses AI to analyse the readout.Credit: Fukuda“We want to expand this AI model for use in annual health checkups for healthy people here in Japan,” says Sasano. “But to do this, we need new data from healthy people, and we have started a prospective cohort study with a local government in Shizuoka City, Japan. We’re using our AI-ECG to run 10-second ECGs in this general population.”To optimize clinical utility, the AI output is categorized into four risk-stratified classes: low, moderate low, moderate high and high. This four-class system is based on likelihood-ratio stratification using predefined cutoff values, notes Wu1.“Because an ECG captures structural and electrophysiological information about the heart beyond rhythm alone, it could serve as a broader diagnostic tool,” says Wu. “We’re hopeful that we can expand our model beyond a single disease by leveraging the richness of these ECG signals. The key challenge will be validating these signals rigorously and translating them in a clinically actionable way.”“Ultimately, we want to use AI to reduce clinical workload and support patient-centred care,” adds Sasano. “Our app can be easily integrated into ECG machines across Japan at low cost, and it is easy to use and understand. Gaining clear risk assessments in seconds will be a groundbreaking development for preventing and treating atrial fibrillation.
Learn more about the research on AI-powered ECG in clinical practice.
